description: Muscle pain (myalgia) and extreme tiredness that does not improve with rest. Common in infections, overexertion, and chronic conditions.
key_questions: How many hours of sleep are you getting?
What is the quality of your sleep — do you wake up feeling refreshed?
Have you done any intense physical activity recently?
Are you under significant stress?
How much water do you drink daily?
Do you eat a balanced diet with fruits and vegetables?
Have you been feeling sad, hopeless, or lost interest in things?
Do you spend most of your day sitting?
When was your last blood test?
Are you taking any medications?
dos: Rest 7-9 hours of quality sleep nightly
Stretch gently — yoga, light stretching helps muscle stiffness
Stay hydrated — 2-3 liters of water daily
Take warm baths with Epsom salt — relaxes muscles
Eat iron-rich foods: spinach, lentils, red meat, beans, dates
Get 15-20 minutes of sunlight daily for vitamin D
Practice stress management: meditation, deep breathing
Take paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain
Eat magnesium-rich foods: almonds, bananas, dark chocolate
Walk for 20-30 minutes daily — low-intensity exercise reduces fatigue
Get Vitamin B12 and Vitamin D levels checked if fatigue persists
donts: Do NOT push through extreme fatigue with caffeine — it masks the problem
Do NOT do intense exercise when already exhausted
Do NOT skip meals
Do NOT sit in one position for hours — move every 30-60 minutes
Do NOT use screens before bed — blue light disrupts sleep
Do NOT consume excess sugar — causes energy crashes
Do NOT ignore fatigue lasting more than 2 weeks
Do NOT self-diagnose chronic conditions
home_remedies: Epsom salt bath: 2 cups in warm bath for 20 minutes — magnesium absorption relaxes muscles
Turmeric-ginger tea: anti-inflammatory, reduces muscle soreness
Ashwagandha powder in warm milk: Ayurvedic adaptogen for fatigue and stress
Warm sesame oil massage: improves circulation and reduces muscle tension
Dates and almonds: natural energy boosters
dietary_guidelines: Eat: spinach, lentils, eggs, nuts, bananas, sweet potatoes, lean proteins, whole grains
Drink: water, green tea, turmeric milk, fresh fruit juices, coconut water
Avoid: processed foods, excess sugar, excessive caffeine, alcohol, junk food
warning_signs: Shortness of breath with mild activity

description: Passage of loose or watery stools 3 or more times daily. Main danger is dehydration. Acute diarrhoea lasts 1-2 days, persistent lasts 2-4 weeks.
key_questions: How many times have you had loose stools today?
When did it start?
Is there any blood or mucus in your stool?
What did you eat in the last 24-48 hours?
Have you eaten street food or outside food recently?
Are you able to keep fluids down?
Have you traveled recently?
Are you taking any antibiotics?
Is your urine dark-colored or reduced?
Has anyone else who ate the same food gotten sick?
dos: Drink ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution) — WHO's first-line treatment
Sip clear fluids frequently: water, coconut water, clear broth
Follow the BRAT diet: Bananas, Rice, Applesauce, Toast
Eat small frequent meals with bland foods
Wash hands thoroughly with soap after every toilet use
Take zinc supplements — WHO recommends zinc for faster recovery
Eat probiotics: yogurt, kefir — restores gut bacteria
Eat cooked, peeled foods only — nothing raw during illness
Monitor urine color — pale yellow means adequate hydration
Make homemade ORS: 1 liter water + 6 tsp sugar + 1/2 tsp salt
donts: Do NOT consume dairy (except yogurt) — lactose worsens diarrhea
Do NOT eat spicy, fried, or fatty foods
Do NOT drink caffeine — increases bowel motility
Do NOT drink alcohol
Do NOT eat raw vegetables during active diarrhea
Do NOT drink fruit juices — high sugar worsens diarrhea
Do NOT take loperamide if you have bloody stool or fever
Do NOT ignore signs of severe dehydration
home_remedies: Homemade ORS: 1L water + 6 tsp sugar + 1/2 tsp salt — lifesaving
Banana: binding food that replaces lost potassium
Pomegranate juice: astringent properties help firm stools
Cumin-fenugreek water: boil together, strain, drink warm
Curd rice (dahi chawal): probiotic + binding starch
Raw papaya: contains papain enzyme that aids digestion
dietary_guidelines: Eat: bananas, white rice, toast, boiled potatoes, clear soups, yogurt, applesauce, curd rice
Drink: ORS, coconut water, clear broth, chamomile tea, water in small sips
Avoid: milk, cheese, spicy food, fried food, caffeine, alcohol, raw vegetables, beans, fruit juice
warning_signs: Diarrhoea lasting more than 3 days in adults
Severe dehydration: dizziness, rapid heartbeat, no urine
Signs of severe dehydration in children/elderly

description: Reflex action to clear airways. Acute cough lasts less than 3 weeks. Chronic cough lasts more than 8 weeks. Can be dry (non-productive) or wet (with mucus).
key_questions: Is your cough dry or are you producing mucus/phlegm?
What color is the mucus — clear, white, yellow, green, or blood-streaked?
How long have you had this cough?
Is the cough worse at night, morning, or during exercise?
Do you smoke or vape?
Are you exposed to dust, pollution, or chemicals at work?
Do you use air conditioning frequently?
Do you have acid reflux or heartburn?
Are you taking any blood pressure medications?
Do you have allergies or asthma?
Have you had COVID-19 recently?
dos: Stay well-hydrated — warm fluids thin mucus and soothe the throat
Drink honey in warm water — WHO recommends honey for cough in adults
Use a humidifier at night
Elevate your head while sleeping — reduces postnasal drip
Cover mouth when coughing — use elbow
Gargle with warm salt water
Steam inhalation with eucalyptus oil for 10 min
Avoid irritants: smoke, dust, strong perfumes
Take cough suppressants (dextromethorphan) ONLY for dry cough at night
For wet cough, take expectorants (guaifenesin) to help clear mucus
Drink tulsi-ginger-honey tea — traditional and effective
donts: Do NOT smoke or vape under any circumstances
Do NOT suppress a wet/productive cough — you need to expel mucus
Do NOT drink very cold water or icy drinks
Do NOT take antibiotics for a viral cough
Do NOT ignore a cough lasting more than 3 weeks
Do NOT lie flat at night — use an extra pillow
Do NOT use cough suppressants for a wet cough — only for dry cough
Do NOT stay in dusty or smoky environments
home_remedies: Honey-ginger: 1 tbsp honey + grated ginger — natural cough suppressant
Tulsi tea: boil 8-10 basil leaves in water with honey
Mulethi (licorice) decoction: boil mulethi sticks in water — soothes airways
Black pepper and honey: mix 1/4 tsp black pepper with 1 tbsp honey
Steam inhalation: add 2 drops eucalyptus oil to hot water, inhale 10 min
Garlic milk: boil 2 crushed garlic cloves in milk — antimicrobial
dietary_guidelines: Eat: honey, ginger, garlic, warm soups, pineapple (bromelain), almonds, turmeric
Drink: warm water, herbal teas, warm milk with turmeric, warm lemon-honey water
Avoid: cold drinks, ice cream, dairy (if it increases mucus for you), fried foods, smoking

condition: Eye Strain (Digital Eye Strain / Computer Vision Syndrome)
description: Eye fatigue from intense use, most commonly caused by prolonged screen use. Digital eye strain affects over 50% of regular computer users.
common_causes: not blinking enough during screen use
key_questions: How many hours per day do you spend on screens (computer + phone + tablet)?
Do you take breaks from screens? How often?
Do you use your phone/laptop in a completely dark room?
When was your last eye exam?
Do you wear glasses or contacts? Is the prescription current?
How far is your monitor from your eyes?
Do your eyes feel dry, gritty, or watery?
Do you get headaches after prolonged screen use?
How much time do you spend outdoors daily?
Do you use any blue light filtering?
dos: Follow the 20-20-20 rule: every 20 min, look at something 20 feet away for 20 sec
Position monitor 20-28 inches from eyes, slightly below eye level
Use artificial tears (lubricant eye drops) if eyes feel dry
Blink deliberately and frequently during screen use
Take a 10-minute break from screens every hour
Adjust screen brightness to match room lighting
Use anti-glare screen protectors
Increase text size on all devices
Spend at least 1 hour outdoors daily — natural light is vital for eye health
Get eye exams every 1-2 years
Use blue light filtering glasses for evening screen use
Keep room well-lit — never work in complete darkness
donts: Do NOT use screens in a completely dark room
Do NOT hold phone closer than 12 inches from face
Do NOT read for hours without breaks
Do NOT skip wearing prescribed glasses
Do NOT use screens 1 hour before sleep
Do NOT rub eyes excessively — can cause irritation and infection
Do NOT ignore persistent blurry vision — get an eye exam
Do NOT stare without blinking — consciously blink every 4-5 seconds
home_remedies: Cucumber slices: place cooled cucumber on closed eyes for 10 min — reduces puffiness
Rose water: soak cotton pads in rose water, place on eyes for 10 min
Palming: rub palms together to warm them, cup over closed eyes for 2 min
Cold tea bags: chilled used tea bags on eyes — tannins reduce swelling
Eye exercises: slowly roll eyes clockwise then counterclockwise 10 times
Warm compress: warm damp cloth over eyes for 5 min — helps dry eyes
dietary_guidelines: Eat: carrots, sweet potatoes, spinach, eggs, fish (omega-3), blueberries, almonds, bell peppers
Drink: water (hydration prevents dry eyes), green tea (antioxidants)
Avoid: excessive screen time, working in dark rooms, smoking (damages retinal blood vessels)
warning_signs: Persistent blurry vision not improving with rest
Floaters, flashes of light, or dark spots

description: Physical manifestations of anxiety and chronic stress including chest tightness, rapid heartbeat, difficulty breathing, insomnia, and digestive issues. Very common and often mistaken for cardiac or respiratory conditions.
key_questions: Are you feeling overwhelmed, anxious, or panicky?
Is your heart racing or do you feel chest tightness?
Are you having difficulty sleeping or staying asleep?
What is causing you the most stress right now?
How much caffeine do you consume daily?
How many hours of sleep are you getting?
How much time do you spend on social media?
Have you had panic attacks before?
Are you eating and exercising regularly?
Do you have anyone to talk to about how you're feeling?
dos: Practice 4-7-8 breathing: inhale 4 sec, hold 7 sec, exhale 8 sec
Exercise regularly — even 20 min daily walk reduces anxiety significantly
Maintain a consistent sleep schedule — same bedtime and wake time daily
Limit caffeine to 1-2 cups max — caffeine triggers anxiety symptoms
Practice progressive muscle relaxation: tense and release each muscle group
Journal your thoughts — writing down worries reduces their intensity
Spend time in nature — even 20 minutes outdoors reduces cortisol
Talk to someone you trust about how you feel
Limit social media to 30 minutes daily
Practice mindfulness meditation — even 5 minutes daily helps
Do grounding exercises: name 5 things you see, 4 you hear, 3 you touch, 2 you smell, 1 you taste
donts: Do NOT consume caffeine if you feel anxious — it makes it worse
Do NOT isolate yourself — social connection is protective
Do NOT doom-scroll social media before bed
Do NOT use alcohol to cope — it worsens anxiety long-term
Do NOT ignore persistent anxiety lasting weeks — seek professional help
Do NOT skip meals — blood sugar drops trigger anxiety
Do NOT catastrophize — challenge negative thoughts
Do NOT self-medicate with drugs or excessive alcohol
home_remedies: Ashwagandha: Ayurvedic adaptogen shown to reduce cortisol levels
Chamomile tea: natural anxiolytic (anti-anxiety) — drink before bed
Lavender inhalation: clinically shown to reduce anxiety symptoms
Warm bath with Epsom salt: magnesium relaxes muscles and mind
Brahmi (Bacopa): traditional memory and anxiety herb — take as supplement
dietary_guidelines: Eat: dark chocolate, berries, nuts, fatty fish (omega-3), yogurt (gut-brain axis), whole grains, bananas, avocados
Drink: chamomile tea, green tea (L-theanine), warm milk, water
Avoid: caffeine, alcohol, processed sugar, highly processed foods, energy drinks
warning_signs: Anxiety preventing you from working or daily activities
Thoughts of self-harm or suicide — call emergency helpline immediately
Persistent chest pain (rule out cardiac cause)
Inability to sleep for more than 3 days

description: Inflammation of the nasal passages caused by an immune reaction to airborne allergens like pollen, dust mites, pet dander, or mold.
key_questions: When do your symptoms get worse — morning, night, or specific seasons?
Is the nasal discharge clear and watery or thick and colored?
Do your eyes itch or water?
Do you have pets at home?
Is your home dusty? When was it last deep-cleaned?
Do the symptoms improve when you go outside or get worse?
Do you have asthma or eczema?
Does anyone in your family have allergies?
dos: Use saline nasal spray to flush allergens from nasal passages
Take antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) — non-drowsy options for daytime
Keep windows closed during high pollen season
Wash bedsheets weekly in hot water to kill dust mites
Shower and change clothes after being outdoors during pollen season
Use air purifiers with HEPA filters indoors
Wear sunglasses outdoors to protect eyes from pollen
Use dust-mite-proof covers on pillows and mattresses
donts: Do NOT dry clothes outside during pollen season
Do NOT open windows on high-pollen days
Do NOT let pets sleep on your bed if you're allergic
Do NOT skip allergy medication on symptom-free days during pollen season
Do NOT rub itchy eyes — use cold compress instead
Do NOT use perfumes or scented products that trigger symptoms
home_remedies: Steam inhalation with eucalyptus oil: clears nasal passages
Neti pot with saline: Ayurvedic nasal irrigation — clinically effective
Local honey: 1 tsp daily — some evidence suggests it builds pollen tolerance
Turmeric milk: anti-inflammatory, reduces allergic response
Peppermint tea: contains menthol that acts as a natural decongestant
dietary_guidelines: Eat: local honey, garlic, ginger, turmeric, citrus fruits, green leafy vegetables, fish (omega-3)
Drink: peppermint tea, green tea, ginger tea, nettle tea
Avoid: alcohol (worsens congestion), dairy (may increase mucus), processed foods
warning_signs: Thick green/yellow discharge lasting more than 10 days (sinusitis)
Symptoms significantly affecting quality of life

description: Pain in the upper or lower back. Lower back pain is the leading cause of disability worldwide. Most cases are mechanical (muscle strain or posture-related) and resolve within 2-4 weeks.
symptoms: pain or stiffness in the back
key_questions: Where exactly is the pain — upper back, lower back, or one side?
Does the pain radiate to your legs, buttocks, or arms?
When did the pain start? Was there a specific event?
Does the pain get worse with sitting, standing, or walking?
How many hours per day do you sit for work or study?
What does your work chair/desk setup look like?
Do you exercise regularly?
How is your mattress — is it old or sagging?
Is there any numbness or tingling?
dos: Keep moving — gentle walking is better than bed rest for back pain
Apply ice for first 48 hours (15 min on, 15 off), then switch to heat
Practice cat-cow stretches and gentle yoga
Maintain proper posture — ears over shoulders over hips
Use an ergonomic chair with lumbar support
Sleep on your side with a pillow between your knees
Strengthen core muscles — planks, bridges, bird-dogs
Take paracetamol or ibuprofen for acute pain
Get up and stretch every 30 minutes during desk work
Maintain healthy body weight — excess weight strains the back
donts: Do NOT stay in bed for more than 1-2 days — prolonged rest weakens muscles
Do NOT lift heavy objects with your back — bend your knees
Do NOT slouch — use lumbar support
Do NOT sit for more than 30 minutes without standing
Do NOT sleep on your stomach — stresses the spine
Do NOT twist your back while lifting
Do NOT ignore pain radiating to legs with numbness — possible nerve issue
home_remedies: Hot water bottle or heating pad: apply to the painful area for 15-20 min
Epsom salt bath: relaxes muscle spasms
Ginger-turmeric paste: apply warm paste on the back for anti-inflammatory effect
Mustard oil massage: warm mustard oil massage improves circulation
Cat-cow stretch: gentle spine mobilization — do 10 reps morning and night
dietary_guidelines: Eat: anti-inflammatory foods — fatty fish, turmeric, ginger, berries, leafy greens, nuts
Drink: water, turmeric milk, ginger tea, cherry juice (anti-inflammatory)
Avoid: processed foods, excess sugar, alcohol, smoking (reduces blood flow to spinal discs)
warning_signs: Pain with bowel or bladder dysfunction — emergency
Pain after a fall or accident
Unexplained weight loss with back pain
Pain that doesn't improve after 4-6 weeks
